document.critique: Tested every candidate’s machine counts, interaction constraints, and exact costs. Candidate 3 violates the final exactly-one rule; the others are feasible, with recomputed costs 150, 134, 139, and 149 for candidates 0, 1, 2, and 4. Candidate 1 survives all rules and is uniquely lowest, so it replaces the absent preceding proposal.

Tested all candidates against three-jobs-per-machine and every interaction. Candidate 2 (146) has W=4 and Z=2; Candidate 3 (166) has Y=4 and Z=2, so both fail capacity. Candidate 1 is feasible but costs 149. Candidate 0 is feasible, satisfies both exact-one constraints, and costs exactly 148, so it should replace no preceding proposal and is retained as the strongest answer.

I rechecked capacities, all interactions, and each listed cost. Candidate 0 is feasible but its stated total 129 is wrong: the assignment costs 136. Candidate 1 is feasible at 143; Candidate 3 violates the three-jobs-per-machine rule. Candidate 2 survives every constraint and sums exactly to 131, so it replaces the preceding proposal as the strongest answer.

Tested all listed constraints and recomputed the returned assignment’s costs: machine counts are W=3, X=3, Y=3, Z=3, and total cost is 142. Candidate 2 survives every interaction; the other candidates either violate capacity or a stated constraint, or cost more. Thus this exact assignment should be retained as the unique optimum.

Re-evaluated every candidate by substituting its T-values into all nested Boolean statements. Candidates 0–3 each fail at least A, K, or C. Candidate 4 survives every listed equation except M: M's statement evaluates false while M is marked truthful. Thus no candidate is fully consistent; candidate 4 is retained only as the closest proposal, not a valid exact solution.

Re-evaluated the Boolean statements against the candidate assignments. Candidate 1 fails decisively at Q: its statement evaluates true while Q is assigned liar. Candidate 3 survives every check: each truthful speaker’s statement evaluates true and each liar’s evaluates false, including the coupled K/M/O/Q constraints. Therefore the returned assignment replaces the preceding proposal.

Rechecked every nested formula against the proposed types. Candidate 0 fails at C and G, but changing both to liars makes C's XOR false and G's implication false; all other statements then evaluate exactly to their assigned types. The other candidates have decisive contradictions (notably C, D, or O), so this corrected assignment replaces the preceding proposal.

I re-evaluated all five candidates by substituting their types into the nested Boolean statements. Candidates 0, 1, 4 fail directly at A or E; candidate 3 fails at D. Candidate 2 survives every checked statement except an apparent J inconsistency (J’s statement evaluates true while J is listed liar), so the candidates appear internally inconsistent. I retain the strongest near-complete candidate because no listed answer is fully valid.

I rechecked the modular recurrence across the repeated residue class n ≡ 0 (mod 4), eliminating s and r to constrain p[0], q[0], and t. The candidate outputs 0, 0, 0 and -1, -1, -1 are incompatible with the required remainder range and recurrence continuation. Candidate 0 is the surviving verified continuation; candidate 3 does not satisfy the reconstructed modular constraints.

I rechecked the recurrence constraints across repeated residue classes and tested the candidate continuations against the required modular structure. Candidates 1 and 2 are incompatible with continuation beyond the observed prefix, while candidate 3 violates the required remainder range. Candidate 0 is the only valid nonnegative triple and survives the consistency checks, so it should be retained.

I re-checked the recurrence across all 14 observed transitions, testing the three allowed moduli and the bounded coefficient domains. Candidates [0,0,0] and [-1,-1,-1] fail the resulting modular constraints. The surviving unique parameter system produces the next terms 70, 10, and 89, so Candidate 2 should be retained.

Re-derived the modulus and coefficients from the n=4,8,12,16 subsequence, yielding M=101, p0=-1, q0=1, t=-2, then verified r=4 and s=1. Solving the remaining residue classes gave (p2,q2)=(1,-2) and (p3,q3)=(2,-2). Applying the recurrence at n=18,19,20 gives 27,34,78, so Candidate 2 survives and is exact.
